title: Cat Non-Healing Sore on Nose or Ear: Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Persistent sores on a cat's nose or ear can be serious. Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A non-healing sore on a cat's ears or nose should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24–48 hours to rule out serious conditions like skin cancer. Seek immediate care if the lesion is bleeding heavily, is painful, or is affecting your cat's appetite.

What are the signs and causes of a non-healing sore on my cat?

A non-healing sore is a lesion, scab, or ulcer that fails to improve after 7 to 10 days or continues to grow and bleed. In cats, these are most commonly found on the bridge of the nose, the nostrils, or the tips of the ears. These areas have thin fur and less pigmentation, making them particularly vulnerable to environmental damage and cellular changes. Potential causes range from persistent bacterial or fungal infections to more serious conditions like Squamous Cell Carcinoma (a common form of skin cancer in felines).

How urgent is it if my cat has a sore that will not heal?

  • The urgency level for a non-healing sore is classified as Medium; while rarely an immediate ER emergency, it should not be ignored.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours for a professional evaluation.
  • Seek care sooner if the sore is bleeding profusely, seems extremely painful, or your cat has stopped eating.
  • Delaying treatment for persistent lesions can lead to the loss of ear tissue or the spread of malignant cells.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's sore for the veterinarian?

  • Taking a clear, well-lit, high-resolution photo of the affected area is one of the most helpful steps in the triage process.
  • A photo allows your veterinarian to assess the margins of the sore and check for signs of infection versus tissue growth.
  • Visual documentation helps determine how quickly the lesion is progressing and serves as a vital clinical baseline.
  • These images help the medical team decide whether your cat needs a routine check-up or a priority biopsy appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, non-healing sores on the nose or ear should raise suspicion for several conditions. Squamous cell carcinoma can present on the pinnae, particularly in early stages, and is associated with ultraviolet light exposure. Feline proliferative and necrotizing otitis externa, a rare disease of unknown cause, can affect the concave aspect of the pinna and external aural orifice, characterized by thick hyperkeratotic crusts covering erythematous plaques that may erode or ulcerate, and may extend to the preauricular, periocular, and perioral regions. Middle-aged to older cats are predisposed to benign and malignant ear canal tumors, presenting with unilateral chronic otic discharge and head shaking.

Chapter: Oncology, Otolaryngology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 534)

faqs.question: Could my cat have skin cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes, Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) is a common cause of non-healing sores on the ears and nose, especially in white or light-colored cats who have had sun exposure.

faqs.question: Should I apply Neosporin or other human ointments?
faqs.answer: No. Many human topical medications contain ingredients like bacitracin or polymyxin B that can be toxic to cats if ingested, and some can even cause anaphylaxis in rare cases.

faqs.question: How will the vet diagnose the sore?
faqs.answer: The vet may perform a 'fine needle aspirate' or a 'punch biopsy' to look at the cells under a microscope. They may also perform a skin scraping to rule out mites or fungal infections like ringworm.
